title: Cold-shock protocol to bleach Aiptasia
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"6jpp9","text":"This protocol describes how to use a short-term cold shock to render Aiptasia aposymbiotic.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"61ff6","text":"After the cold shock it includes two possibilities to proceed one faster one with usage of DCMU and a slower and more gentle one with only keeping the animals in the dark.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"fh8fh","text":"Preparing DCMU (3-(3,4-dichlorophenyl)-1,1-dimethylurea) stock solution:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":72}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9vkhf","text":"0.0313 M DCMU in 95-100% ethanol.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2rm7o","text":"For 50 ml, dissolve 7.3 mg in 50 ml ethanol.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"d8lq8","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"c261o","text":"For an end concentration of 50 µM add 32 µl of the stock to 200 ml of artificial seawater. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"504i5","text":"Fluorescence filters:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fk8iu","text":"Every wavelength between 400 and 550 nm efficiently axcites the algal chlorophyll.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"eq2mn","text":"The chlorophyll fluorescence emission peaks between 650 and 720 nm.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9o8jq","text":"This means that most RFP filter sets with a wide bandpass or longpass emission filter at above 600 nm will work well.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1oifh","text":"GFP longpass filter sets will work as well but only in combination with an RGB camera to distinguish red algal fluorescence and green autofluorescence of the anemones themselves.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"dovdk","text":"These numbers are only rough estimations based on a few algal strains. For purchasing new filter sets it will be still best to test them before buying.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"e3087","text":"DCMU is toxic and suspected to be carcinogenic.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":47}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"28vlv","text":"https://chemicalsafety.com/sds1/sdsviewer.php?id=30234487\u0026name=3-(3%2C4-Dichlorophenyl)-1%2C1-dimethylurea","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":106}],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"1B4943475AB84B30B61A4B7ADE4A813D","url":"https://chemicalsafety.com/sds1/sdsviewer.php?id=30234487\u0026name=3-(3%2C4-Dichlorophenyl)-1%2C1-dimethylurea"}}}}
steps: Prepare a tank of anemones. It is best (but not critical) if you let them sit in the tank until they stick to the sides/bottom of the tank.
Chill a large volume of seawater to 4°C.
Pour out the seawater in the Aiptasia tank and replace it with the 4°C seawater. Place the entire tank into a 4°C refrigerator (or cold room).

Remove the tank from 4°C, pour out the cold water, and replace with room-temperature seawater. The animals will be spitting out boluses of algae, which you will see as dark brown balls next to the anemones
Repeat the water change 2-3 times by pouring out the tank water and replacing it with room-temperature seawater.
Place the tank into darkness (we use dark cabinets) at room temperature for 1 – 2 days. Continue to replace seawater with room-temperature seawater once a day.
You now have two options: a faster method or a slower method. The faster method works quickly but is harsher on the anemones; the slower method is more gentle on the anemones but takes longer.

Prepare seawater containing DCMU (or ‘diuron’: 3-[3,4-dichlorophenyl]-1,1-dimethylurea). We generally use a final DCMU concentration in seawater between 10 μM and 50 μM. Higher concentrations work faster, but are harsher on the anemones so you may need more frequent water changes.

Leave tank in complete darkness with regular room-temperature seawater. Continue to change the water every 1 – 2 days. Feed animals at least once a week, but 2 – 3 times a week if possible: whenever you feed, change the water the next day.
Remove the tank from the dark cabinet. Replace tank seawater with the DCMU-seawater. Incubate at room temperature in constant light for several weeks.

Replace the water with DCMU-seawater once or twice a week (depending on how the anemones look: when they appear very unhappy, replace the water). Feed anemones with brine shrimp at least once a week; more feeding is better, so feed 2 – 3 times a week if possible. Importantly, after each feeding the water must be changed the next day.

You can start screening the anemones under a fluorescence stereoscope after 2 – 3 weeks to check for the presence of algae. Continue incubating with DCMU until anemones appear aposymbiotic. See guidelines section for information about fluorescence filters.

When anemones appear aposymbiotic, incubate first a few of them in room-temperature seawater in the light (without DCMU) for 1 week and then check for algal presence under a fluorescence stereoscope. Tthis step ensures there are no algae still in the anemones: if any are present, they will populate quickly over the week and become detectable. If they still contain algae, continue the DCMU treatment. If they remain free of algae, they are fully aposymbiotic.
You can start screening the anemones under a fluorescence stereoscope after 1.5 months to check for the presence of algae. Continue incubating in the dark until anemones appear aposymbiotic.

When anemones appear aposymbiotic, incubate first a few of them in room-temperature seawater in the light for 1 week and then check for algal presence under a fluorescence stereoscope. This step ensures there are no algae still in the anemones: if any are present, they will populate quickly over the week and become detectable. If they still contain algae, continue the dark treatment. If they remain free of algae, they are fully aposymbiotic.
creator: Christian CR Renicke
